name: LangChain / LangGraph
architecture_paradigm: State graph (cyclic directed graph with state machine)
persistence: Yes (built-in checkpointing, durable execution, typed StateGraph with Pydantic schemas; LangGraph Cloud for managed persistence)
strengths: State machine model handles cycles/loops natively
Durable execution with checkpointing (crash recovery)
Typed state schema with compile-time validation
Largest ecosystem: 100+ model providers, extensive integrations
Production-proven: Klarna, Uber, LinkedIn, Replit
Human-in-the-loop at granular checkpoints
LangSmith/LangGraph Cloud for observability and managed runtime
weaknesses: Steep learning curve (requires graph-thinking mental model)
High API surface area; many abstractions to learn
Debugging cyclic graphs is harder than linear chains
LangChain's rapid API churn historically caused migration pain
Cloud offering vendor lock-in risk
use_cases: Enterprise agents with human-in-the-loop approval gates
Any workflow with branching, looping, and retry logic

name: LlamaIndex
architecture_paradigm: Data-centric pipeline (load, index, query — DAG via workflows engine)
multi_agent: Yes (Workflows engine for event-driven multi-agent orchestration; LlamaAgents)
streaming: Yes (streaming responses from query engines and agent loops)
persistence: Yes (index-based state; LlamaCloud managed indexing/retrieval; vector store persistence)
strengths: Best-in-class data connectors: 160+ sources via LlamaHub
Superior document parsing with LlamaParse (OCR, tables, charts)
Agentic Document Processing (parse, extract, split, classify)
Multiple index types: vector, keyword, tree, knowledge graph
Structured output extraction from unstructured documents
Evaluation framework for RAG pipeline quality
LlamaCloud managed service for enterprise
weaknesses: Primarily data/RAG-centric; general agent orchestration less mature
Agent capabilities not as deep as LangGraph or CrewAI
Workflows engine newer and less battle-tested
Heavy focus on document processing may not suit non-RAG use cases
Learning curve for advanced index configurations
use_cases: Question answering over large document corpora
Agentic RAG with tools and structured output
Data extraction from PDFs, spreadsheets, and presentations

name: AutoGen (Microsoft) / AG2
multi_agent: Yes (native multi-agent with 9+ orchestration patterns; group chat, swarms, nested chats, sequential)
streaming: Yes (streaming of agent responses and tool calls)
persistence: Yes (conversation history context; AG2 has session management; AG2 AgentOS for shared state)
strengths: Pioneered multi-agent conversation patterns
Rich orchestration: 9 patterns including group chat, swarms, nested chats
Strong research heritage and academic backing
Human-in-the-loop built into agent primitives
A2A and MCP protocol support in AG2
Cross-framework interoperability (LangChain, OpenAI, ADK agents)
AG2 AgentOS for unified runtime
weaknesses: Original AutoGen in maintenance mode (Microsoft shifted to MAF)
AG2 community fork has smaller ecosystem and fewer stars
Conversational model can be harder to control for deterministic workflows
Debugging multi-agent conversations is complex
Documentation split between two projects causes confusion
use_cases: Collaborative problem-solving with multiple specialist agents
Coding agents with code review and iteration

name: CrewAI
architecture_paradigm: Role-based agent teams (sequential, hierarchical, parallel task execution)
multi_agent: Yes (native — define crews with roles, goals, backstories, and tools per agent)
streaming: Limited (streaming support improving; Flows enable event-driven streaming)
persistence: Yes (process-oriented state; CrewAI Enterprise with tracing, dashboards, logging)
strengths: Most intuitive multi-agent abstraction: role, goal, backstory
Fastest path from idea to working multi-agent prototype
Zero dependency on LangChain (pure Python)
60% Fortune 500 adoption; 2B+ agentic executions
CrewAI Flows for event-driven orchestration
Excellent documentation and 100k+ certified developers
Strong enterprise traction (PwC, IBM, NVIDIA, Capgemini)
weaknesses: Less control over agent internals vs LangGraph
Opinionated architecture; not ideal for all patterns
No built-in checkpointing for long-running workflows
Enterprise features require paid platform
Agent-to-agent communication is task-output mediated, not direct messaging
Performance depends heavily on underlying model
use_cases: Marketing content pipelines (research → write → edit → publish)

name: Haystack (deepset)
architecture_paradigm: Modular pipeline / DAG (directed acyclic graph of components)
multi_agent: Yes (agent workflows with tool use; multi-step agent pipelines)
streaming: Yes (streaming chunks for generators and pipelines)
persistence: Yes (pipeline state; vector store integrations for document memory; conversation memory)
strengths: Mature production-grade RAG and search framework
Modular pipeline design with explicit control over every component
Strong enterprise focus: SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001
Excellent multi-modal support (text, vision, audio)
Comprehensive evaluation and debugging tooling
Apache 2.0 license with enterprise platform available
Used by Netflix, Airbus, LEGO, European Commission
weaknesses: Agent capabilities less mature than dedicated agent frameworks
Pipeline DAG model not ideal for cyclic/looping agent workflows
Primarily Python-only (no TypeScript/Go SDKs)
Steeper learning curve for advanced agent patterns
Smaller community than LangChain
use_cases: Document QA and knowledge base applications

name: Dify
architecture_paradigm: Visual workflow builder (DAG with branching, parallel execution, conditional nodes)
multi_agent: Yes (agent framework with ReAct/Function Calling; multi-agent workflows via visual builder)
streaming: Yes (streaming via API; real-time agent execution output)
persistence: Yes (application state; conversation memory; knowledge base persistence; LLMOps logging)
strengths: Visual drag-and-drop workflow builder (low-code/no-code)
All-in-one: RAG pipeline, agent framework, prompt IDE, observability
50+ built-in tools and plugins marketplace
100+ LLM providers supported including local Ollama
MCP native integration
Self-hostable via Docker Compose with enterprise features
Backend-as-a-Service APIs
1M+ apps in production, used by Maersk, Novartis
weaknesses: Visual builder limits flexibility for complex agent logic
Not ideal for developers who prefer code-first approach
Self-hosting requires significant infrastructure
Less suited for low-level agent orchestration control
Heavier resource footprint than library-based frameworks
use_cases: Internal copilots and enterprise knowledge agents
Customer support automation with escalation paths
Document review and invoice processing pipelines
Teams needing no-code/low-code AI app development

name: Coze (ByteDance)
architecture_paradigm: Visual agent builder (workflow-based, multi-agent pipelines, no-code/low-code)
multi_agent: Yes (multi-agent pipelines with handoffs; specialized bots passing tasks)
streaming: Yes (streaming via API and bot interactions)
persistence: Yes (managed cloud state; knowledge bases via RAG; conversation memory)
github_stars: Coze Studio OSS (growing); platform itself is SaaS
strengths: Generous free tier (100 workflow runs/day)
60+ built-in plugins: web search, image gen, code execution, calendar
Multi-channel deployment: Telegram, Discord, Slack, LINE, web widget
Visual builder accessible to non-developers
ByteDance infrastructure and Doubao LLM integration
MCP protocol support
Plugin ecosystem for extensibility
weaknesses: ByteDance ownership raises data sovereignty concerns
Limited customization vs code-first frameworks
Vendor lock-in to Coze/ByteDance ecosystem
Less suitable for complex production agent orchestration
International version lags behind Chinese version in features
use_cases: Rapid bot building with minimal coding
Customer-facing chatbots across multiple messaging platforms
Teams needing quick deployment with generous free tier

name: Semantic Kernel (Microsoft)
architecture_paradigm: Plugin-based orchestration (Kernel with plugins, functions, memory, and agent framework)
multi_agent: Yes (Agent Framework GA in 2025/2026; multi-agent orchestration with specialist agents)
streaming: Yes (streaming via kernel functions and agent responses)
persistence: Yes (built-in memory system; vector DB integrations for RAG; plugin state)
strengths: Enterprise-grade: telemetry, hooks, filters, security, audit logging
Multi-language: C#, Python, Java (.NET first-class)
Native integration with Azure ecosystem and Microsoft 365 Copilot
Plugin architecture combining native code + semantic prompts
Process Framework for business process modeling
Model-agnostic with 20+ vector DB integrations
Spans kernel → agent → process → multi-agent
weaknesses: C#/.NET focus may alienate Python-first developers
Optimized for Azure ecosystem; less neutral than alternatives
Smaller community than LangChain
Documentation can be overwhelming with many concepts
Python SDK lags behind C# in maturity
use_cases: Enterprise copilots and Microsoft ecosystem agents
Business process automation with structured workflows
Enterprise teams needing observability and governance

name: Agno (formerly Phidata)
architecture_paradigm: Multi-agent team framework (agent teams with memory, knowledge, reasoning)
multi_agent: Yes (Agent Teams with collaboration, chain-of-thought reasoning, shared context)
streaming: Yes (streaming for agent responses and real-time output)
persistence: Yes (built-in storage drivers; 20+ vector DB integrations; structured conversation memory)
strengths: High performance: agents initialize in microseconds, low memory for simple tasks
23+ LLM providers supported (OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, etc.)
Built-in Agent UI for chatting with and observing agents
Async-first design for high throughput
Pre-built AI product templates
Built-in observability and monitoring
FastAPI deployment support
weaknesses: Smaller community than LangChain or CrewAI
Rebrand from Phidata caused confusion
Documentation still maturing
Less production-proven than older frameworks
Ecosystem smaller than LangChain

name: Swarm (OpenAI)
architecture_paradigm: Handoff-based (linear agent-to-agent delegation via tool calls)
multi_agent: Yes (handoffs between agents; agent networks for coordination)
persistence: No (context variables only; no built-in persistence — ephemeral)
strengths: Simple, elegant handoff model
Lightweight: very few abstractions, fast to prototype
Clean agent-to-agent delegation as typed tool calls
Easy to test and evaluate
Excellent for learning multi-agent patterns
weaknesses: Experimental/educational — not production-ready
Replaced by OpenAI Agents SDK (no longer maintained)
No built-in persistence or checkpointing
Linear handoff chains, not arbitrary graph topologies
No support for complex orchestration patterns
OpenAI-only models
use_cases: Learning and experimenting with multi-agent handoff patterns

name: A2A (Google Agent-to-Agent)
architecture_paradigm: Communication protocol (not a framework — enables cross-framework agent interoperability)
multi_agent: Yes (purpose-built for multi-agent discovery, delegation, and cross-vendor collaboration)
streaming: Yes (via SSE for task status updates and artifact streaming)
persistence: Yes (task lifecycle: submitted → working → completed → failed; push notifications for state changes)
strengths: Industry-standard protocol for cross-vendor agent communication
Donated to Linux Foundation (vendor-neutral governance)
150+ supporting organizations (AWS, Microsoft, Salesforce, SAP)
Based on existing web standards: HTTP, JSON-RPC 2.0, SSE
Agent Cards for capability discovery
Production SDKs in 5 languages
Complements MCP (MCP=agent-to-tool, A2A=agent-to-agent)
weaknesses: Protocol only — not a framework; needs implementation
Fewer client libraries than MCP
Missing governance/business-context attributes in base spec
Cross-framework implementations still maturing
Requires agents from different vendors to adopt the protocol
use_cases: Agent service mesh across organizational boundaries
Standardizing agent communication in multi-vendor environments
Agent discovery and delegation at scale

name: OpenAI Agents SDK
architecture_paradigm: Imperative agent loop with handoff chains (Agents with tools, handoffs, guardrails, tracing)
multi_agent: Yes (handoffs, agents-as-tools, sandbox agents for long-running tasks)
streaming: Yes (streaming for agent responses and tool call outputs)
persistence: No (no built-in state persistence; sessions are managed externally; Temporal integration for durable execution)
strengths: Cleanest handoff model in the ecosystem
Three-tier guardrails (input, output, tool) running in parallel
Built-in tracing with OpenAI Traces dashboard
Voice agent support with interruption detection
Lightweight: very few abstractions
Provider-agnostic (supports 100+ LLMs)
Sandbox agents for containerized long-running work
weaknesses: No built-in state persistence (you manage checkpointing)
Handoffs are linear chains, not arbitrary graph topologies
No native A2A support
Voice features lock you into OpenAI-specific models
Relatively young ecosystem
Guardrails can be complex to configure
use_cases: Simple to moderately complex agent workflows
Multi-agent systems with explicit handoff delegation
Applications needing built-in guardrails and safety

name: Cursor Agent
architecture_paradigm: IDE-native agent (VS Code fork with AI as first-class primitive; agent mode, composer, background agents)
multi_agent: Yes (subagents, background agents, best-of-n multi-model execution, parallel agent windows)
streaming: Yes (real-time streaming of code edits, completions, agent reasoning in UI)
persistence: Yes (codebase indexing for semantic search; session context; .cursorrules for project configuration)
github_stars: ~80k+ (product; not primarily a GitHub OSS project; proprietary)
strengths: Best-in-class inline autocomplete and tab-to-accept flow
Multi-file Composer edits with full codebase awareness
Agent Mode for autonomous task execution
Background Agents and cloud sandbox agents
/best-of-n runs same task across multiple models simultaneously
MCP integration for external tools
Privacy Mode for code security
weaknesses: Proprietary and expensive at scale (credit-based billing)
Long agent sessions degrade reasoning quality
Monorepos without .cursorignore cause indexing issues
Credit consumption can be surprising during heavy usage
Internet connection required for all AI features
Vendor lock-in to Cursor ecosystem

name: Devin (Cognition)
architecture_paradigm: Autonomous coding agent (planning → execution → debugging → PR submission in sandboxed VM)
multi_agent: No (single autonomous agent — can hand off to humans via Slack/PR)
streaming: Yes (real-time streaming of agent actions, terminal output, browser interaction in UI)
persistence: Yes (long-running session state in sandboxed VM; integrated with GitHub/GitLab/Jira/Linear/Sentry)
strengths: Fully autonomous: plans, codes, tests, debugs, submits PRs
Sandboxed VM with browser, terminal, and editor access
Can learn unfamiliar technologies by reading docs
Integrates with GitHub, Jira, Linear, Sentry for end-to-end workflows
Self-correcting: fixes build errors and iterates on failures
Used by Goldman Sachs, Infosys, Endava
weaknesses: Expensive: originally $500/mo, now $20/mo (Core) but still costly per seat
Inconsistent execution on complex tasks
Context retention degrades in long sessions
No direct code access while agent works (back-and-forth friction)
Most effective on well-scoped repetitive tasks only
Enterprise-focused: no free tier
use_cases: Autonomous bug fixing and feature implementation

name: MetaGPT
architecture_paradigm: SOP-based multi-agent (simulates software company roles: PM, architect, engineer, QA)
multi_agent: Yes (multi-role software company simulation; role-based task decomposition and collaboration)
streaming: Limited (focused on structured document and code output rather than streaming)
persistence: Yes (project state: generates user stories, API specs, architecture docs, code in workspace)
strengths: Unique 'software company in a box' approach
Generates comprehensive outputs: user stories, competitive analysis, API specs, code
Role-based decomposition of complex software tasks
Low cost: ~$0.20 per example, ~$2.0 for full project
SOP(Team) → Code formula produces coherent results
MGX platform extends to collaborative interactive development
weaknesses: Narrowly focused on software development scenarios
Output quality depends heavily on underlying model capability
Less flexible for non-software development agent tasks
Limited streaming support
Research-oriented; less production-tested as general agent framework
use_cases: Multi-role task decomposition for complex projects
Rapid prototyping of software projects from requirements

name: AutoGPT
architecture_paradigm: Autonomous ReAct loop (goal decomposition → subtask execution → iteration)
multi_agent: Partially (now has platform for continuous agents; primarily single-agent autonomous)
streaming: Yes (real-time streaming of agent reasoning, actions, and results)
persistence: Yes (continuous agent state on AutoGPT Platform; long-running background agents)
strengths: Most popular autonomous agent project on GitHub
Pioneered the autonomous agent paradigm
Mature platform with visual builder and agent marketplace
Long-running continuous agents (not just one-shot)
Massive community and extensive plugin ecosystem
Low-code agent builder accessible to non-developers
Self-hostable via Docker
weaknesses: Originally a demo that burned through API credits
Less structured than newer frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI)
Quality of autonomous execution inconsistent
Platform approach adds complexity vs library-based frameworks
Resource-intensive for continuous agents
use_cases: Continuous background agents for automated workflows

name: Google ADK (Agent Development Kit)
architecture_paradigm: Agent class/loop (agents with tools, multi-agent composition, graph workflows in ADK 2.0)
multi_agent: Yes (sequential, parallel, loop, hierarchical multi-agent composition; collaborative agents in 2.0)
streaming: Yes (streaming via ADK Web Dev UI and API for agent outputs and events)
persistence: Yes (artifact management; memory/state backings via Service Registry; Vertex AI Memory Bank)
github_stars: ADK Python ~8k (multiple language repos: Python, TS, Go, Java, Kotlin)
strengths: Official Google framework for Vertex AI and Gemini
Multi-language: Python, TypeScript, Go, Java, Kotlin (cross-language parity)
Built-in evaluation framework (evalsets, LLM-as-judge scoring)
ADK Web Dev UI for local debugging and testing
First-class A2A protocol integration
One-command deploy to Vertex AI Agent Engine
Graph workflows in ADK 2.0
MCP integration
weaknesses: Optimized for Gemini/Google Cloud (model preference)
Smaller community than LangChain or CrewAI
Relatively new (v1.0 GA April 2026); ecosystem still growing
ADK 2.0 graph workflow feature recently released
Critical security vulnerability history (CVE-2026-4810)
use_cases: Production agents on Google Cloud/Vertex AI
Enterprise agents needing evaluation and governance

name: Pydantic AI
architecture_paradigm: Type-safe agent framework (agent class with dependency injection, tools, streaming, graphs)
multi_agent: Yes (multi-agent orchestration; A2A protocol integration for agent-to-agent)
streaming: Yes (streamed structured output with immediate validation; streaming event-based communication)
persistence: Yes (durable execution for long-running agents; DBOS, Prefect integrations; graph state)
strengths: Type safety: Pydantic validation guarantees structured outputs
Model-agnostic: supports virtually every model provider
Seamless observability with Pydantic Logfire (OpenTelemetry)
Durable execution: agents preserve progress across crashes/restarts
Human-in-the-loop tool approval
Graph support via type hints for complex workflows
MCP, A2A, and multiple UI event stream standards
YAML/JSON agent definition (no code required)
weaknesses: Very new framework (still early development)
Smaller ecosystem and community than mature frameworks
Documentation still developing
Not yet production-proven at massive scale
Python-only (no TypeScript/Go/C# support)
use_cases: Financial and regulated domain agents requiring correctness
